title: Beer on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Beer contains malt tannins, hop resins, proteins (from barley), and sugars. On upholstery, the tannin component stains while the sugar creates a sticky residue that attracts dirt. Fresh beer stains are light and water-soluble. Dried beer stains darken as the tannins oxidize and the sugars caramelize, leaving a stiff, discolored patch.
whyThisWorks: Surfactant treatment dissolves the sugar and protein components on upholstery. The acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) targets the tannin discoloration. Beer stains are among the easier tannin stains because the tannin concentration is relatively low compared to wine or coffee.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.instruction: Find the tag sewn into a seam under a cushion. One letter: W = water-safe, S = solvent only, WS = both, X = vacuum only. On S or X, stop and call a pro — water will ring the fabric worse than beer ever could. On W or WS, proceed. No tag? Treat as S and escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Beer is a tannin stain — malt tannins, hop resins, plus sugar that will attract future dirt if you don't get it out. Alkaline products (ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most all-purpose upholstery sprays) lock tannin into the fiber permanently. Use only cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% peroxide, and white vinegar.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + blot the fresh spill
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab a drop of Dawn + 3% peroxide solution on the back of the cushion or hidden frame. Blot with a white cloth after two minutes — if the fabric holds color, you're safe. Meanwhile, press a stack of paper towels or white cloth straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work outside-in until the cloth stops picking up beer.

Dip a white cloth in cold water, wring nearly dry, press on the stain. Lift, rinse the cloth in cold water, press again. Repeat 3–4 times. You're diluting the beer and flushing sugar out of the weave before the real chemistry starts. Never pour water on upholstery — foam absorbs what you can't see, and water rings form at the damp-zone edge.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide — damp cloth only
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of Dawn with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a bowl. Dip a white cloth, WRING NEARLY DRY, and press on the stain. Let it sit 5 minutes, then blot with a clean damp cloth. Peroxide oxidizes the tannin; Dawn lifts it and cuts the sugar. Most beer stains lift in 1–2 cycles — beer has less tannin than wine or coffee.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On dark or patterned upholstery, skip this step unless the patch test was clean.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar with one cup of cold water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, dab the area. Vinegar neutralizes peroxide residue and prevents the tannin from drifting toward brown as it dries. Blot dry with a fresh cloth.

Blot until no more moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Point a room fan at the area for 4–8 hours. Good airflow prevents mildew in the cushion foam and stops water rings. No hair dryer — heat sets tannin and can damage the fabric.

homeSolutions.agent: Daylight inspect — repeat if shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Inspect in natural light. Beer rings can reappear as a tan halo when fully dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 once more. Two rounds is the home ceiling. If a shadow persists, the tannin has set — escalate.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional upholstery cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If care code is S or X, the patch test failed, or the stain survived two rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Beer on [W/WS/unknown] upholstery, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide + vinegar rinse. No alkaline products, damp-cloth only." That sends them straight to commercial tannin formula + low-moisture extraction.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No over-wetting — causes water rings and backing damage
No chlorine bleach on colored fabric
Always check care code (W, S, WS, X) before treating
fiberSensitivities: Mixed fiber blends common — treat for the most sensitive fiber present
Cushion foam absorbs moisture — avoid saturation
Test in hidden area before treating visible sections

escalation.when: If beer stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is beer on upholstery, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
